div.p: Standard accumulation loses O(√n) bits of precision because each addition rounds the result. For 768 dimensions, this costs ~5 bits. Kahan summation tracks the rounding error (c) and subtracts it from the next term, recovering most lost precision. Cost: 4x more operations, but still faster than using f64. Rule of thumb: Use Kahan for dimensions > 512 where distance comparisons must be accurate to 1e-5 or better.

g.text: t = sum + y; // Alas, sum big, y small
c = (t - sum) - y; // Recover lost bits!
